symptoms: Uniform deep green colouring across the leaf
No spots, lesions, or discolouration
Firm, turgid leaf texture with no wilting
Normal leaf size and shape for the crop type
treatment: Maintain regular, consistent watering schedule
Ensure adequate sunlight exposure (6-8 hours for most crops)
Continue balanced NPK fertilization as per crop calendar
Monitor weekly for early signs of pest or disease
prevention: Inspect new plants before introducing them to the field
Keep soil well-drained to prevent root stress
Remove weeds that can harbour pests or pathogens
Maintain proper plant spacing for airflow
organic_remedies: None needed — plant is currently healthy
Apply compost or vermicompost to maintain soil health
chemical_remedies: If soil test shows deficiency, apply appropriate micronutrient supplement

symptoms: Olive-green to dark brown velvety spots on upper leaf surfaces
Corresponding lighter spots on leaf undersides in early stages
Yellowing and premature leaf drop
Scabby, cracked, corky lesions on fruit surface
treatment: Apply Myclobutanil or Captan fungicide from early green tip stage
Remove and rake up fallen scabby leaves
Prune the canopy to improve airflow
Spray at 7-10 day intervals during rain-splash periods
prevention: Clear all fallen leaves and fruit every autumn
Apply dormant copper spray before bud break
organic_remedies: Remove and compost fallen leaves away from orchard

symptoms: Purple spots on leaves that enlarge
Black, sunken lesions on fruit
Cankers on branches
Premature leaf drop
treatment: Remove infected fruit and branches
Apply captan or myclobutanil fungicide
Prune dead wood in winter
Sanitize pruning tools between cuts

symptoms: Orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Fuzzy brown projections on leaf underside
Premature leaf drop
Bright orange gelatinous galls on junipers
treatment: Apply myclobutanil or mancozeb fungicide
Remove nearby juniper/cedar trees if possible
Prune and destroy infected leaves
Remove fallen leaves in autumn
prevention: Prune juniper galls before spring rains
Avoid planting apple trees near cedars
Apply protective fungicides in early spring

symptoms: Uniform green colour across all leaves
No lesions, spots, or abnormal markings
Healthy fruit development with smooth skin
Strong branch structure with no cankers
treatment: Continue regular irrigation and fertilization schedule
Monitor for early signs of pest activity
Maintain proper pruning practices
prevention: Regular sanitation of fallen fruit and leaves
Maintain proper tree spacing for air circulation
organic_remedies: Apply compost around drip line annually
Use beneficial insects for pest control
chemical_remedies: Apply preventive dormant oil if historically prone to pests

symptoms: Bright green to blue-green leaves
Healthy berry clusters with good colour
No leaf spots or discolouration
Sturdy cane growth with no dieback
treatment: Maintain acidic soil pH (4.5-5.5)
Apply ammonium-based fertilizers
Provide consistent moisture, especially during fruiting
prevention: Mulch with pine needles or wood chips
Prune out old canes after harvest
organic_remedies: Apply sulphur to maintain soil acidity
Use composted pine bark as mulch

symptoms: White to greyish powdery clusters on leaves
Leaf curling and stunting
Yellowing of older leaves
Premature leaf drop in severe cases
treatment: Spray sulfur-based fungicide (2g/L)
Apply neem oil (5ml/L)
Remove infected branches and leaves
Improve air circulation through pruning
prevention: Improve air circulation with proper spacing
Space plants properly for good airflow
Water at base, avoid wetting foliage
organic_remedies: Baking soda solution (1 tbsp per litre)

symptoms: Glossy green leaves with smooth margins
Healthy bark with no gumming or cankers
Good fruit set with normal colour development
No wilting or leaf curl
treatment: Annual dormant pruning in late winter
Maintain consistent soil moisture
Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring
prevention: Clean up fallen fruit and leaves
Protect from bird damage with netting
organic_remedies: Use kaolin clay for pest prevention

symptoms: Small, rectangular brown lesions parallel to leaf veins
Lesions develop into tan or grey spots and streaks
Reduces photosynthetic area significantly
Chlorosis (yellowing) around lesions
Premature leaf death from bottom up
treatment: Apply fungicide timely at first sign
Increase plant spacing for better airflow
Rotate with non-host crops (soybeans, wheat)
Tillage to bury crop residue
prevention: Clear debris to avoid overwintering spores
Plant early to avoid humid late-season conditions
organic_remedies: Apply compost tea as foliar spray

symptoms: Small brown pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules become powdery and reddish-brown
Leaves turn yellow and die early
Reduced kernel fill and yield loss
Pustules rupture releasing orange-brown spores
treatment: Apply fungicide at first sign of rust
Increase plant spacing for air circulation
Harvest early if severe infection occurs
Remove and destroy heavily infected plants
prevention: Plant early to avoid late-season humidity
Apply preventive fungicide in high-risk areas
organic_remedies: Remove lower leaves to improve airflow

symptoms: Long, tan cigar-shaped lesions (2.5-15 cm)
Grayish-green spots that turn brown
Lesions run parallel to leaf veins
Premature drying of leaves from bottom up
Severe yield loss when upper leaves infected
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin or Pyraclostrobin at tasseling
Rotate with non-grass crops for 2 years
Manage irrigation to reduce leaf wetness
Tillage to bury infected crop residue
prevention: Rotate crops with soybeans or wheat
organic_remedies: Compost or bury crop debris deeply

symptoms: Deep green, upright leaves with no lesions
Healthy tassel and ear development
Strong stalk with no discolouration
No pustules or spots on any plant parts
treatment: Maintain consistent soil moisture
Apply nitrogen at V6 and tasseling stages
Monitor for European corn borer
Ensure proper plant population density
organic_remedies: Use cover crops for soil health
chemical_remedies: Apply pre-emergence herbicide if weed pressure high

symptoms: Circular tan lesions with dark borders on leaves
Black mummified fruit that shrivels
Black pycnidia (pimple-like structures) in lesions
Red-brown spots on young shoots
Fruit rot that spreads rapidly in warm, wet weather
treatment: Apply Myclobutanil or Mancozeb at 7-10 day intervals
Remove mummified fruit from vines and ground
Prune infected canes back to healthy wood
Improve canopy air circulation
Remove and destroy all infected clusters
prevention: Maintain open vine canopy through pruning
Apply fungicide from bud break through harvest
organic_remedies: Complete removal of infected plant parts

symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern on leaves (chlorotic bands)
Dark red or yellow borders on leaf lesions
Black spots on berries (measles)
White rot in trunk and cordons
Sudden vine collapse in severe cases
Small, black pimples on affected wood
treatment: Prune out infected wood back to healthy tissue
Protect pruning wounds with fungicide paste
Remove severely infected vines completely
Improve overall vine vigour with proper nutrition
No curative treatment once symptoms appear
prevention: Delay pruning until late winter to early spring
Apply wound protectants after each cut
Use clean planting material from certified sources
Remove infected vines promptly to prevent spread
organic_remedies: Sanitize pruning tools between cuts (10% bleach)
Remove infected vines and burn away from vineyard
chemical_remedies: Wound sealants with fungicide (flusilazole or tebuconazole)
Sodium arsenite (banned in many countries, use alternatives)
No effective systemic fungicide for existing infections

symptoms: Dark brown irregular spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-brown velvety growth on leaf underside
Premature leaf drop starting from lower leaves
Reduced fruit quality and sugar content
Defoliation that exposes fruit to sunburn
treatment: Apply copper-based or Zineb fungicide immediately
Remove infected leaves from vine and ground
Improve canopy management and air flow
Avoid overhead irrigation
Apply second spray 10-14 days after first
prevention: Good vine spacing and annual pruning
Apply preventive fungicide spray before symptoms
Remove and destroy fallen leaves in autumn
organic_remedies: Remove and compost infected leaves away
Apply neem oil (5 ml/L) as preventive

symptoms: Healthy green vines with uniform colour
Well-formed grape clusters with proper colour
No leaf spots, lesions or deformities
Smooth bark with no cankers or gumming
treatment: Regular care including pruning and training
Maintain proper irrigation and fertility
Monitor for pests weekly
Thin clusters if necessary for quality
prevention: Apply dormant oil before bud break
Keep ground clear of fallen fruit

symptoms: Yellow shoots (blotchy mottle pattern on leaves)
Lopsided, small, bitter fruits that stay green
Premature fruit drop
Stunted growth and twig dieback
Zinc deficiency-like symptoms on leaves
Off-tasting, misshapen fruit unsuitable for sale
treatment: Remove and destroy infected trees immediately
Control Asian citrus psyllid vector with insecticides
Apply foliar nutrients to maintain tree health
No cure available - prevention is critical
Use certified disease-free nursery stock
prevention: Use reflective mulch to repel psyllids
Apply systemic insecticides to young trees
Quarantine protocols for moving plant material
organic_remedies: Remove and destroy infected trees completely
Release Tamarixia radiata (parasitic wasp for psyllids)
Use kaolin clay to deter psyllids
Apply neem oil for psyllid control
Install yellow sticky traps for monitoring
chemical_remedies: No treatment for HLB once infected - remove tree

symptoms: Small yellow-green spots on young leaves
Dark water-soaked spots on older leaves
Spots with straw-coloured centers
Lesions on fruit that become raised and scabby
Defoliation in severe cases
Leaf edges may curl and die
treatment: Copper-based bactericide spray every 5-7 days
Remove infected foliage immediately
Avoid overhead watering
Apply fixed copper + mancozeb for better control
Destroy severely infected plants
prevention: Crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops (3+ years)
Control weeds that can harbour bacteria
Avoid working with plants when wet

symptoms: Dark green, smooth leaves with no spots
Healthy fruit set with good colour
Strong stem structure
No wilting or yellowing
treatment: Consistent watering (avoid fluctuations)
Balanced NPK fertilization
Support plants with stakes or cages
Mulch to maintain soil moisture
prevention: Monitor for aphids and other pests
organic_remedies: Use drip irrigation to keep leaves dry

symptoms: Dark brown target-like ring spots on lower leaves
Yellowing (chlorosis) around lesions
Premature defoliation starting from bottom
Lesions on stems and tubers in severe cases
Reduced tuber size and yield
treatment: Apply Mancozeb or Azoxystrobin at 7-day intervals
Remove infected debris from field
Balance nitrogen fertilization (avoid excess)
Harvest promptly when mature
Destroy crop residue after harvest
prevention: Crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops (3-4 years)
organic_remedies: Neem oil spray (5 ml/L water) weekly
Apply compost tea as foliar spray

symptoms: Dark, water-soaked leaf patches that enlarge rapidly
White cottony sporulation on leaf undersides in humid conditions
Blackened stems with greasy appearance
Rapid plant collapse within 7-10 days
Brown, dry rot on tubers
Entire field can be destroyed quickly
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil) immediately at first sign
Destroy infected haulm before tuber infection
Harvest promptly in dry conditions after killing vines
Do not compost infected plant material
Store tubers in cool, dry conditions
prevention: Apply preventive fungicide before symptoms appear
Avoid irrigation in cloudy, humid weather
Hill soil high to protect tubers
Destroy volunteer potatoes and cull piles
organic_remedies: Complete removal and burning of infected plants

symptoms: Deep green, healthy leaves with no spots
Sturdy stems with good height
Uniform tuber development
No wilting or yellowing
treatment: Regular irrigation (1-2 inches per week)
Hill soil when plants are 6-8 inches tall
Monitor for Colorado potato beetle
Harvest when vines die back naturally
organic_remedies: Use straw mulch to conserve moisture

symptoms: Uniform green trifoliate leaves

No leaf spots, rust pustules, or wilting
Strong stem with no lodging

Monitor for soybean aphids
Apply nitrogen-fixing inoculant if needed
Harvest when pods are brown and dry

symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves

Leaf defoliation starting from bottom

Remove infected leaves and plants promptly
Avoid overhead irrigation
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 2-3 years
Sanitize stakes and cages between seasons
prevention: Use disease-free seeds or treated seeds
Hot water treatment of seeds (122°F for 25 min)

symptoms: Dark brown concentric ring spots (target-board pattern) on older lower leaves
Yellow (chlorotic) halo surrounding each lesion
Lesions first appear on the oldest, lowest leaves and progress upward
Severe cases cause early defoliation and reduced fruit yield

treatment: Apply Mancozeb (2 g/L) or Chlorothalonil fungicide spray every 7-10 days
Remove and destroy all visibly infected leaves immediately
Apply copper-based fungicide as a protective spray
Avoid overhead irrigation — water at the base of the plant
Improve canopy airflow by staking or pruning lower leaves
prevention: Rotate tomato crops with non-solanaceous crops every 2-3 years
Use certified disease-free transplants or seeds
Maintain adequate nitrogen — over-fertilisation increases susceptibility
Mulch soil to reduce splash-dispersal of fungal spores
Water in the morning so foliage dries quickly
organic_remedies: Neem oil spray (5 ml per litre of water) every 5-7 days
Copper oxychloride (OMRI-listed) as a protective spray
Baking soda solution (1 tbsp per litre) to raise leaf pH
Remove heavily infected leaves and compost away from the field
Apply compost tea as foliar spray

symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to brown velvety mould on lower leaf surface
Leaves curl, wilt, and die prematurely
Flowers and fruit may be affected in severe cases
Spreads rapidly in high humidity (85%+)
treatment: Improve air circulation immediately
Reduce humidity by spacing plants and pruning
Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves
Water at base, never overhead
prevention: Use drip irrigation to keep leaves dry

symptoms: Small, circular spots with dark borders and light centers
Black specks (pycnidia) visible in lesion centers
Yellow halos around spots
Lower leaves affected first, moving upward
Severe defoliation from bottom up
Fruit not directly affected but sunscald occurs
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb at 7-10 day intervals
Remove infected leaves immediately
Mulch to prevent soil splash
Avoid overhead watering
Destroy crop residue after harvest
prevention: Space plants for good air circulation
Stake and prune to keep leaves off ground

symptoms: Dark brown target-like concentric rings on leaves

Leaf yellowing and defoliation
Small, sunken spots on green fruit
Rapid spread in warm, wet conditions
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide
Remove and destroy infected plant parts
Improve air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation
Rotate with non-host crops

symptoms: Severe stunting of plant growth
Upward curling and cupping of leaves
Yellow leaf margins and interveinal yellowing
Reduced leaf size and flower drop
Fruit set greatly reduced or absent
Leaves become thick and leathery
Entire plant appears bushy due to shortened internodes
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whitefly vector with insecticides
No cure once infected - prevention is key
Use reflective mulches to repel whiteflies
Plant tolerant varieties if available
prevention: Install fine mesh netting (50 mesh) over young plants
Apply reflective silver mulch to repel whiteflies
Monitor for whiteflies with yellow sticky traps
Remove infected plants within 5 days of symptoms
organic_remedies: Remove and destroy ALL infected plants
Use neem oil for whitefly control (5 ml/L)
Apply kaolin clay (Surround) to deter whiteflies
Yellow sticky traps for monitoring and control
chemical_remedies: Imidacloprid 70 WG @ 0.5 g/L (soil drench for young plants)
Buprofezin 25 SC @ 1 ml/L water (for whitefly control)
No treatment for the virus itself - remove infected plants

symptoms: Mottled light and dark green pattern on leaves
Leaf curling and distortion
Stunted plant growth
Mosaic patterns on fruit (uneven ripening)
Fern-like leaf development in severe cases
Reduced yield and fruit quality
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants
Sanitize hands and tools between plants (milk solution 20%)
No cure once infected
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops
Wait at least 2 weeks before replanting
prevention: Use disease-free seed (hot water treat at 122°F for 25 min)
Wash hands with milk before handling plants (protein denatures virus)
Disinfect tools in 20% powdered milk solution
Avoid tobacco use around tomatoes (TMV from tobacco)
organic_remedies: Milk spray (20% solution) on tools and hands
Do not compost infected plant material
chemical_remedies: No chemical control for the virus itself
Remove and destroy infected plants only
Some evidence that systemic acquired resistance (SAR) inducers may help
